author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
The Sixth Item of the Accusation, and Pelagius’ Reply.
title: The Sixth Item of the Accusation, and Pelagius’ Reply.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Chapter 16 [VI.]—The Sixth Item of the Accusation, and Pelagius’ Reply.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: The next objection was to the effect that Pelagius in that same book of his wrote thus: “A man is able, if he likes, to be without sin;” and that writing to a certain widow he said, flatteringly: “In thee piety may find a dwelling-place, such as she finds nowhere else; in thee righteousness, though a stranger, can find a home; truth, which no one any longer recognises, can discover an abode and a friend in thee; and the law of God, which almost everybody despises, may be honoured by thee alone.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: And in another sentence he writes to her: “O how happy and blessed art thou, when that righteousness which we must believe to flourish only in heaven has found a shelter on earth only in thy heart!”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: In another work addressed to her, after reciting the prayer of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, and teaching her in what manner saints ought to pray, he says: “He worthily raises his hands to God, and with a good conscience does he pour out his prayer, who is able to say, ‘Thou, O Lord, knowest how holy, and harmless, and pure from all injury and iniquity and violence, are the hands which I stretch out to Thee; how righteous, and pure, and free from all deceit, are the lips with which I offer to Thee my supplication, that Thou wouldst have mercy upon me.’”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: To all this Pelagius said in answer: “We asserted that a man could be without sin, and could keep God’s commandments if he wished; for this capacity has been given to him by God.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: But we never said that any man could be found who at no time whatever, from infancy to old age, had committed sin: but that if any person were converted from his sins, he could by his own labour and God’s grace be without sin; and yet not even thus would he be incapable of change ever afterwards.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: As for the other statements which they have made against us, they are not to be found in our books, nor have we at any time said such things.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Upon hearing this vindication, the synod put this question to him: “You have denied having ever written such words; are you therefore ready to anathematize those who do hold these opinions?”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Pelagius answered: “I anathematize them as fools, not as heretics, for there is no dogma.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: The bishops then pronounced their judgment in these words: “Since now Pelagius has with his own mouth anathematized this vague statement as foolish verbiage, justly declaring in his reply, ‘That a man is able with God’s assistance and grace to be without sin,’ let him now proceed to answer the other heads of accusation against him.”
